prompt:
Our QA team replays minesweeper sessions from screenshots and I keep doing the "which cells are forced" analysis by hand. I want that automated: a SOLVER that takes a partially revealed board and reports every cell that is provably a mine and every cell that is provably safe. Not a game engine — no reveals, no flood fill, no state; one pure analysis call. Files: minesolver.h and minesolver.c, C17, stdlib/string headers only, no I/O, no heap.

    int mines_solve(const char *const *rows, int nrows, int ncols,
                    int *mines_out, int *nmines,
                    int *safe_out, int *nsafe);

Board encoding: rows is nrows NUL-terminated strings of exactly ncols chars. '?' = unrevealed, 'M' = an already-confirmed mine (we flagged it in an earlier pass), '0'..'8' = a revealed count of adjacent mines (all 8 neighbors, diagonals included).

The deduction rules — exactly these two, nothing fancier:

1. Single square. Each digit cell contributes one constraint: among its '?' neighbors, exactly (digit minus adjacent 'M' count) are mines. If that remainder is 0, every '?' neighbor is safe; if it equals the number of '?' neighbors, every one is a mine.
2. Subset. For two constraints where one's cell set is a PROPER subset of the other's: the difference cells (big minus small) must contain exactly (big remainder minus small remainder) mines — so if that equals the size of the difference they are all mines, and if it is zero they are all safe.

Apply both rules over and over, feeding results back in (a deduced mine removes itself from every constraint and decrements its remainder; a deduced safe cell just drops out of the sets), until a full pass adds nothing. The fixpoint is order-independent, so don't worry about rule ordering — just iterate until quiet.

Output: mines_out gets every NEWLY deduced mine (never the pre-marked 'M' cells), safe_out every deduced-safe cell, both as flat indices row*ncols+col in ASCENDING order, counts through nmines/nsafe. Cells the rules can't decide stay unlisted — this tool must never guess. Both arrays are caller-allocated with room for nrows*ncols entries.

Return 0 on success. Return -1 (and don't trust the outputs) for garbage input — any NULL pointer, nrows or ncols outside [1,16], a row whose strlen isn't ncols, a character outside the alphabet above — and also when the board is CONTRADICTORY: if at any point a constraint's remainder goes negative or exceeds its remaining '?' cells, the screenshot was misread and I need the -1, not a best effort.

make test is the acceptance gate. Makefile, mintest.h and test_minesolver.c are fixed — hands off.
notes: 7G7 item 3. Differentiates from py-minesweeper/ts-minesweeper per catalog: constraint SOLVER (single-square + subset rules to fixpoint), not an engine. All pinned deduction sets computed by an independent Python oracle implementing the same two rules; every fixture hand-verified to admit a consistent mine assignment matching the deductions (fixture F checked digit-by-digit). The 1-2-3-2-1 and corner fixtures require the subset rule, so a single-square-only implementation fails.
